source.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: Diagnosis and Management

signals: monotherapy with inhaled bronchodilator, stepping up to combination therapy
Pharmacotherapy and smoking cessation are the mainstays of treatment
long-term oxygen therapy, and surgery may be considered
adding inhaled corticosteroids as symptom severity and airflow obstruction progress

extraction.summary: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) should be suspected in patients with risk factors (primarily smoking history) who report dyspnea, chronic cough, or wheezing history. Diagnosis must be confirmed by spirometry showing post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7. Treatment focuses on smoking cessation and pharmacotherapy with inhaled bronchodilators, stepping up to combination therapy and adding inhaled corticosteroids as severity progresses.

population.age_criteria: Age older than 35-45 years with significant smoking history
population.risk_criteria: Smoking history >40 pack-years (strong predictor), α-antitrypsin deficiency, significant exposure to indoor/outdoor air pollution, occupational dusts, or chemicals
population.disease_definition: Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7 confirmed by spirometry
population.exclusions: Asthma, bronchiectasis, congestive heart failure, cystic fibrosis, interstitial lung disease, lung cancer, tuberculosis
population.severity_staging: GOLD 1 (mild, FEV1 ≥80%), GOLD 2 (moderate, 50-79%), GOLD 3 (severe, 30-49%), GOLD 4 (very severe, <30%)

key_thresholds.context: Threshold for long-term oxygen therapy indication

recommendations.recommendation: Start monotherapy with inhaled bronchodilator (LABA or long-acting anticholinergic)
Smoking cessation for all patients who smoke
Add inhaled corticosteroids for FEV1 <50% predicted or frequent exacerbations
recommendations.notes: Typically for GOLD C or D classification

recommendations.recommendation: Long-term oxygen therapy for severe resting hypoxia (PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or O2 sat ≤88%)
recommendations.notes: Use at least 15 hours per day

recommendations.recommendation: Avoid screening for COPD in asymptomatic patients
Long-term oxygen therapy for patients with severe resting hypoxia (PaO2 ≤55 mm Hg or oxygen saturation ≤88%)
recommendations.notes: Use at least 15 hours per day

recommendations.recommendation: Pulmonary rehabilitation for symptomatic patients with FEV1 <50% of predicted
recommendations.notes: Requires at least 6 months to achieve benefits

recommendations.recommendation: Monotherapy with long-acting beta agonist or long-acting anticholinergic for symptomatic patients with FEV1 <60% of predicted
recommendations.notes: Choice based on patient preference, cost, and adverse effects

recommendations.recommendation: Pneumococcal vaccination (PPSV23 and PCV13 one year apart)
recommendations.notes: For patients 19-64 years who smoke or have COPD

red_flags: Severe resting hypoxia (PaO2 ≤55 mm Hg or oxygen saturation ≤88%)
Cor pulmonale or signs of tissue hypoxia
Exercise hypoxemia with PaO2 ≤55 mm Hg or oxygen saturation ≤88%
extraction.follow_up: Periodic evaluation for hypoxemia in moderate to severe COPD patients. Monitor spirometry for disease progression. Assess symptom control and medication effectiveness regularly.
extraction.escalation_path: Escalate to pulmonologist for: severe refractory symptoms, consideration of pulmonary rehabilitation, lung transplant evaluation, or complex medication management. Consider hospitalization for severe exacerbations or respiratory failure.
